2: With the ever-increasing market demands for poultry and do- mestic animal products, large-scale and intensive breeding farms have developed rapidly. Consequently, the large amount of animal dung produced has become a serious environmental pollution problem around the world. For example, the production of 0.618 billion pigs resulted in 405.84 million tonnes of urine and 245.96 million tonnes of manure in China in 2005. These wastes contain toxic concentrated pollutants, including 16.1 million tonnes of biochemical oxygen demand (BOD), 16.4 million tonnes of chemical oxygen demand (COD), 1.28 million tonnes of ammonium nitrogen (NH4-N), 1.05 million tonnes of total phosphorus (TP), and 2.79 million tonnes of total nitrogen (TN). The pollution load of swine

slurry from 0.618 billion pigs is equal to that produced by 8.034 billion people in China (Pei, 2012). Furthermore, residual antibiotics (e.g., fluoroquinolones, sulfonamides, and retracyclines) and path- ogenic bacteria (e.g., Escherichia coli) in land-applied manure pre- sent a potential threat to public and ecological health (Zhao et al., 2010; Millner et al., 2014). Therefore, it is a priority to deal with the waste emissions from breeding farms and reduce the associated environmental pollution.

Anaerobic digestion (AD) is an attractive approach to reduce the environmental impacts of manure management (Gutiérrez et al., 2016). Compared with other methods (e.g., field application of untreated manure, compost, aquatic forage and ash), AD has the significant advantages of producing an energy source and achieving multiple environmental benefits (Ardolino et al., 2018; Martínez- Valdez et al., 2017; Gutiérrez et al., 2016; Lijó et al., 2014). More- over, using digestate as a fertilizer on arable land can replace part of the chemical fertilizer, improve soil properties (Tampio et al., 2015), reduce chemical pollution (Lijó et al., 2014), and enhance nutrient recycling (Edwards et al., 2017; Berglund and Börjesson, 2006).

However, this approach requires large investment and technical and operational expertise, whereas the lack of monitoring mecha- nism for biogas plant construction always results in poor opera- tional management and low productive efficiency (Ragazzi et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2017; Gutiérrez et al., 2016).

With the maturing of AD and biogas production technology, biogas production systems are increasingly being integrated into agricultural production systems (Chen and Liu, 2017). According to the size of the biogas digester, biogas production systems can be divided into large-scale and household systems. Large-scale biogas production (LBP) systems are often used in intensive farming and the effective volume of the biogas digester is 600 m³ or more. LBP is a modern biogas production system that uses mechanization, electrification and automation. The LBP production chain is 'waste-pretreatment-fermentation around 35°Cupitication of biogas-powergeneration-products' and is powered by biogas and electricity (Kan et al., 2015). Household biogas production (HBP) systems are mostly built by families raising 5-10 pigs in the countryside and the biogas digester volume is normally 8-10m3. HBP is a typical traditional biogas production method with a simple production chain, which is 'waste-fermentation at ambient tem- perature-products', and is restricted by environmental factors (Liu et al., 2017).

Numerous studies have investigated the development and environmental performance of HBP (Chen et al., 2017; Chen and Liu, 2017; Wang et al., 2010). An 8-m³ hydraulic biogas digester can mitigate the impacts of environmental pollutant potential in terms of eutrophication (EP, by 85%), acidification (AP, by 54%), energy consumption (EC, by 39%), global warming potential (GWP, by 27%), human toxicity potential (HTP, by 19%), and photochemical oxida- tion (POP, by 6%) based on the world's per capita environmental potential impacts in 2000 (Wang et al., 2010). As for LBP, studies have also evaluated the influence of biogas engineering technology in terms of economic benefits, ecological equivalent value and comprehensive production efficiency (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006; Pei, 2012; Jin et al., 2015). The production scale and complexity of LBP and HBP are markedly different. Increasing the complexity of the system and the use of co-products helps achieve better performance and optimal use of available resources (Agostinho and Ortega, 2012). However, few systematic compari- sons between LBP (centralized production based on machinery) and HBP (dispersed production based on manpower) systems have been undertaken that combine the energy balance and environ- mental impacts with the production conditions and address the advantages and disadvantages of each system.

Life cycle assessment (LCA) is a flexible and comprehensive technique that is used to assess the environmental effects associ- ated with a product, process, or activity (ISO14040, 2006; Cremiato et al., 2017). In this study, an in-depth analysis was performed on the current status of LBP and HBP in Tongchuan, Shaanxi Province using LCA. The main objectives of this study were to: (1) compare LBP with HBP in terms of energy output rate, energy-use efficiency and environmental impact potential in the life cycle of biogas; (2) quantify the practical impacts of biogas production conditions and processes on the energy balance and pollutant emissions of the biogas production systems; and (3) identify the potential of LBP and HBP for optimizing the production method and promoting the development of ecological agriculture in the future.

However, few systematic compari- sons between LBP (centralized production based on machinery) and HBP (dispersed production based on manpower) systems have been undertaken that combine the energy balance and environ- mental impacts with the production conditions and address the advantages and disadvantages of each system.

Life cycle assessment (LCA) is a flexible and comprehensive technique that is used to assess the environmental effects associ- ated with a product, process, or activity (ISO14040, 2006; Cremiato et al., 2017). In this study, an in-depth analysis was performed on the current status of LBP and HBP in Tongchuan, Shaanxi Province using LCA. The main objectives of this study were to: (1) compare LBP with HBP in terms of energy output rate, energy-use efficiency and environmental impact potential in the life cycle of biogas; (2) quantify the practical impacts of biogas production conditions and processes on the energy balance and pollutant emissions of the biogas production systems; and (3) identify the potential of LBP and HBP for optimizing the production method and promoting the development of ecological agriculture in the future.
7: With the ever-increasing market demands for poultry and do- mestic animal products, large-scale and intensive breeding farms have developed rapidly. Consequently, the large amount of animal dung produced has become a serious environmental pollution problem around the world. For example, the production of 0.618 billion pigs resulted in 405.84 million tonnes of urine and 245.96 million tonnes of manure in China in 2005. These wastes contain toxic concentrated pollutants, including 16.1 million tonnes of biochemical oxygen demand (BOD), 16.4 million tonnes of chemical oxygen demand (COD), 1.28 million tonnes of ammonium nitrogen (NH4-N), 1.05 million tonnes of total phosphorus (TP), and 2.79 million tonnes of total nitrogen (TN). The pollution load of swine

slurry from 0.618 billion pigs is equal to that produced by 8.034 billion people in China (Pei, 2012). Furthermore, residual antibiotics (e.g., fluoroquinolones, sulfonamides, and retracyclines) and path- ogenic bacteria (e.g., Escherichia coli) in land-applied manure pre- sent a potential threat to public and ecological health (Zhao et al., 2010; Millner et al., 2014). Therefore, it is a priority to deal with the waste emissions from breeding farms and reduce the associated environmental pollution.

Anaerobic digestion (AD) is an attractive approach to reduce the environmental impacts of manure management (Gutiérrez et al., 2016). Compared with other methods (e.g., field application of untreated manure, compost, aquatic forage and ash), AD has the significant advantages of producing an energy source and achieving multiple environmental benefits (Ardolino et al., 2018; Martínez- Valdez et al., 2017; Gutiérrez et al., 2016; Lijó et al., 2014). More- over, using digestate as a fertilizer on arable land can replace part of the chemical fertilizer, improve soil properties (Tampio et al., 2015), reduce chemical pollution (Lijó et al., 2014), and enhance nutrient recycling (Edwards et al., 2017; Berglund and Börjesson, 2006).

However, this approach requires large investment and technical and operational expertise, whereas the lack of monitoring mecha- nism for biogas plant construction always results in poor opera- tional management and low productive efficiency (Ragazzi et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2017; Gutiérrez et al., 2016).

With the maturing of AD and biogas production technology, biogas production systems are increasingly being integrated into agricultural production systems (Chen and Liu, 2017). According to the size of the biogas digester, biogas production systems can be divided into large-scale and household systems. Large-scale biogas production (LBP) systems are often used in intensive farming and the effective volume of the biogas digester is 600 m³ or more. LBP is a modern biogas production system that uses mechanization, electrification and automation. The LBP production chain is 'waste-pretreatment-fermentation around 35°Cupitication of biogas-powergeneration-products' and is powered by biogas and electricity (Kan et al., 2015). Household biogas production (HBP) systems are mostly built by families raising 5-10 pigs in the countryside and the biogas digester volume is normally 8-10m3. HBP is a typical traditional biogas production method with a simple production chain, which is 'waste-fermentation at ambient tem- perature-products', and is restricted by environmental factors (Liu et al., 2017).

Numerous studies have investigated the development and environmental performance of HBP (Chen et al., 2017; Chen and Liu, 2017; Wang et al., 2010). An 8-m³ hydraulic biogas digester can mitigate the impacts of environmental pollutant potential in terms of eutrophication (EP, by 85%), acidification (AP, by 54%), energy consumption (EC, by 39%), global warming potential (GWP, by 27%), human toxicity potential (HTP, by 19%), and photochemical oxida- tion (POP, by 6%) based on the world's per capita environmental potential impacts in 2000 (Wang et al., 2010). As for LBP, studies have also evaluated the influence of biogas engineering technology in terms of economic benefits, ecological equivalent value and comprehensive production efficiency (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006; Pei, 2012; Jin et al., 2015). The production scale and complexity of LBP and HBP are markedly different. Increasing the complexity of the system and the use of co-products helps achieve better performance and optimal use of available resources (Agostinho and Ortega, 2012). However, few systematic compari- sons between LBP (centralized production based on machinery) and HBP (dispersed production based on manpower) systems have been undertaken that combine the energy balance and environ- mental impacts with the production conditions and address the advantages and disadvantages of each system.

Life cycle assessment (LCA) is a flexible and comprehensive technique that is used to assess the environmental effects associ- ated with a product, process, or activity (ISO14040, 2006; Cremiato et al., 2017). In this study, an in-depth analysis was performed on the current status of LBP and HBP in Tongchuan, Shaanxi Province using LCA. The main objectives of this study were to: (1) compare LBP with HBP in terms of energy output rate, energy-use efficiency and environmental impact potential in the life cycle of biogas; (2) quantify the practical impacts of biogas production conditions and processes on the energy balance and pollutant emissions of the biogas production systems; and (3) identify the potential of LBP and HBP for optimizing the production method and promoting the development of ecological agriculture in the future.
8: This study was carried out in Yijun and Yanchi counties of

Tongchuan City (34°48'-35°34" N, 108°35'-109"9" E), Shaanxi Province, China. Tongchuan is located at the intersection between the Guanzhong Basin and the Loess Plateau in north Shaanxi and has a continental monsoon climate featuring four distinct seasons. Average annual temperature ranges from 8.9 to 12.3 with the minimum of -7 °C in January and the maximum of 29 °C in July. Average annual rainfall is between 555.8 and 709.3 mm, and average annual sunshine duration is 2345.7-2412.5 h. Tongchuan is one of the National Recycling Economy's top ten green develop- ment cities, a pilot city for Resource-style Cities' Sustainable Development and a comprehensive demonstration of fiscal policy on energy conservation and emissions. In this context, Yanchi has been chosen to conduct a pilot study of livestock waste disposal and effective agricultural recycling under the 'Integration and demon- stration of agriculture-fruit-animal husbandry compound cycle technology of the Loess Plateau' project, while Yijun has been developed as a provincial-level ecological garden county based on a sustainable, integrated agricultural model (SIAM).

10: The LBP system in Yanchi is connected to a pig farm with ~8000 pigs a year. Technical overview of LBP is shown in Fig. 1. The plant covers an area of 2160 m², with the performance period of 365 d/yr. It uses a continuous stirred tank reactor (CSTR, a heating system, a combined heat and power system (CHP), and a biogas purification system. All systems work by mechanical transmission, consuming electricity and biogas. The reaction temperature in the CSTR is ~35 Swine slurry (22.58 tonne/d) from the farm flows into the biogas plant through a tunnel for AD that can produce 679 m³ of biogas and 21.77 tonnes of digestate per day on average. Biogas produced by the CSTR flows through a dehydration tank (mechanical, gas-water separation membrane), a desulfurization tank (wet oxidation desulfurization, >99.5% efficiency), and a sec- ond dehydration tank (mechanical, gas-water separation mem- brane) successively for purification. Of the biogas produced, 60% is used for CHP with an electricity generation efficiency of 36% and a heat generation efficiency of 45%, and the heat produced is inter- nally used to heat the digester. In addition, 7% of the biogas is needed to heat up the digester fermentation, and 33% is injected into the biogas grid, providing for ~100 families nearby on average). The electricity production 917 kWh/d and is mainly used in the farm and the biogas plant. The digestate is transported by truck to arable lands in local villages and used as organic fertilizer, with an average transportation radius of km. The fertilization methods include: foliage spraying of processed surface biogas digestate (normally at the rate of 600-1500 kg/ha, depending on the variety and site of the plants); fertigation with biogas digestate (irrigating 30-501 for each apple tree at full productive age and 20-30 kg for each young apple tree in early July, August, and November).

The HBP system chosen is based on the SIAM for plant, animal, and biogas production (Wu, 2015). Fig. 2 shows the structure of the biogas digester in HBP. An 8 m³ biogas digester is built under a 12 pigpen with four pigs each year, and its performance period is 273 d/yr. The biogas digester is made of red brick and Portland cement with a removable cover. Swine slurry is stacked beside the pigpen and moved into the biogas digester by a shovel every other day for AD at ambient temperature. The products are 0.8 m³ of biogas and 0.04 tonnes of digestate per day on average. The biogas is a source of renewable energy for cooking. A purification regulator is connected to the biogas stove for desulfurization through ferric oxide desulfurizer. The digestate is transported by a tractor with a transportation radius of 2 km and used as organic fertilizer for
Fig. 1. Technical schematic diagram of large-scale biogas production (CHP, Combined heat and power system; CSTR, Continuous stirred tank reactor).
Digester size: 300 cm X 180 cm X 160 cm

walnut production (furrow application of 40-50 kg for each tree at full productive age and 4-8 kg for each young tree in early November, and 5 kg for each young tree every 15-30 days during March to August) in the SIAM region.

13: Data were collected over a two-year period (July 2014-July 2016). Specifically, environmental data were taken from the Tong- chuan City Annals (Wu et al., 2015). Operation data of LBP were taken from the plant, and operation data of HBP were obtained through face-to-face conversation with farm owners (Table 1). Manufacturing processes of the plant were confirmed through field

investigation. Samples of swine manure, swine slurry, and biogas digestate were collected in July and October 2015. Laboratory determination of total nutrient and total solid (Table 2) was per- formed at the Northwest Agriculture & Forest University Test Center (Yangling, Shaanxi Province, China). Further details are provided in the Supplementary material S1. In addition, data of pollutant emissions, energy conversion rate, and other coefficients used in the study were collected by literature review (detailed in the text).

framework proposed by the International Organization for Stan- dardization (Wang et al., 2016). The environmental performance of LBP and HBP was assessed following the basic steps, i.e., goal and scope definition, inventory analysis, impact assessment and interpretation.

The goal of LCA is to quantify and compare the energy con- sumption and environmental impacts of LBP and HBP. To compare the biogas production systems, a functional unit (FU) describing the primary function fulfilled by a product system is needed to treat different systems as functionally equivalent (Thomassen et al., 2008). Since the main function of the biogas systems is to pro- duce energy from manure fermentation, 2136 tonne/yr manure flowing into the biogas digester was chosen as the FU of LBP base on its manure processing capacity (equal to ~529 HBPs). In this study, the whole life cycle required for biogas production, from manure leaving the farm to biogas utilization and biogas manure, was considered. The production processes were classified into gath- ering, processing, and use phases (Fig. 3). Transport associated with fertilization was also included. The impacts during the construction and demolition of the systems were not considered here due to the complexity involved with building materials and the lack of basic

Inventory analysis consists of the collection of data on resource use, energy consumption, and pollutant emissions from each phase in the production system (Thomassen et al., 2008). The first step is to determine the feedstock components and manufacturing pro- cesses for each phase. The feedstock for biogas production is swine slurry obtained in raw form. For LBP, manufacturing of swine slurry includes feedstock collection, pretreatment, AD, purification, CHP generation, digestate management and fertilization. For HBP, manufacturing includes feedstock collection, AD, digestate man- agement and fertilization. Further analysis of each phase was car- ried out.

Energy consumption in the LCA of the biogas production sys- items is based on the function of the systems. LBP with a CSTR is complex and functions entirely by mechanical transmission. The energy consumption in the LBP system was estimated using different input and output energy flows, including heating of the feedstock, biogas for CHP, electricity for pumps and mixers, diesel for digestate transport, and heat demand for the reactor, digester and piping. HBP is a traditional biogas production method that depends on manual work and natural fermentation. The energy consumption in the HBP system was estimated mainly using diesel for transport. All energy flows were calculated as uncovered and untransformed natural resources (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006).

Electricity consumption in LBP is based on the CHP generated in the system, assuming that 1 kWh of electricity corresponds to 3.6 MJ of primary energy in the life cycle of LBP. The calorific value of biogas is based on the methane (CH4) content. The calorific value of CH4 is 35.90 MJ/m³; thus, 1 m³ of biogas corresponds to the primary energy of 22.50 I in LBP and 20.57 MJ in HBP. Field investigation showed that the local biogas production was 510-540 tonne-volatile solids (VS) in LBP and 310-380 /tonne-VS in HBP, without consideration of the emissions. These values were similar to the data reported by Wu (2015) in our study area. Thus,
Flow of the system Energy consumption in the system

the biogas production in LBP (~35 °C) was estimated to be 525 m³/ tonne.VS on average. Biogas produced in HBP was different in each season: 375 m³ 3/tonne-VS in summer (~25 °C 312-375 m³/ tonne-VS in spring and fall (10-20°C), and zero in winter (<10 °C). The average biogas production in HBP was estimated to be 343.75 m³ /tonne VS. The heat demand for AD in LBP was estimated according to Kou et al. (2013) and Doms and Mueller (2005). More details are available in the (Supplementary material S2 and Tables S1-3).

The production and distribution of diesel fuel are estimated to correspond to 10% of the energy content in the fuel (Börjesson, 1996); thus, 1 L of diesel corresponds to 42.65 MJ of primary en- ergy (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006; Jin et al., 2015). Manufacturing and maintenance of trucks and tractors used to transport the digestate and for fertilization are assumed to correspond to 8% and 20% of the energy content in the diesel fuel, respectively (Börjesson, 1996). Thus, using 1 L of diesel for transportation by truck in LBP and tractor in HBP corresponds to 46.06 MJ and 51.18 MJ of primary energy, respectively. The energy inputs and outputs during the life cycle of the biogas production systems are summarized in Table 3.
Choice of allocation involves partitioning the environmental impacts of a multifunctional process. Pollutant emissions during

each process can be divided into direct and indirect emissions. The direct emissions occur during production processes, including manure emissions in the gathering phase, biogas released inad- vertently through leaks in the digester and tubing, and digestate emissions during management and after fertilization in the use phase. (1) Manure emissions of carbon dioxide (CO2) and CH4 ac- count for 85.71% and 14.29% of the carbon loss, respectively (Jiang et al., 2011; Chowdhury et al., 2014), and emissions of nitrogen oxide (NOx) and ammonia (NH3) account for 0.76% and 99.24% of the nitrogen loss, respectively (Chowdhury et al., 2014). In total, 5.49% and 5.99% of the initial total carbon and nitrogen are lost from manure to feedstock in the LBP, and 6.87% and 7.93% are lost in the HBP, respectively; (2) Biogas leakage in the processing phase is calculated to be 0.6% (Xu et al., 2013) and 9.2% (Wang, 2012) of biogas production in LBP and HBP, respectively. (3) Digestate emissions of CO2 and CH4 in management account for 36.6% and 61.4% of the carbon loss in the LBP, and 34.5% and 57.3% in the HBP, respectively (digesters can release biogas from the outlets; Bruun et al., 2014), and emissions of NOx and NH3 account for 0.76% and 99.24% of the nitrogen loss, respectively (ventilation has less in- fluence on nitrogen losses; Chowdhury et al., 2014). In total, 0.61% and 6.45% of the initial total carbon and nitrogen are lost from

(2015). The direct emission factors are summarized in Table 4.

The indirect emissions occur during energy consumption, including (1) emissions from the burning of biogas: CO2, CO, vola- tile organic compounds (VOC), nitrogen dioxide (NO2), and NOx in LBP (2.96, 0.40, 1930.42, 2.28, and 0.31 g/mi 3 respectively) and HBP (2.76, 0.77, 1808.71, and 0,59 g/m³, 3 respectively) by referring to Jury et al. (2010) and Wang et al. (2010), and SO2 in the two systems (assumed to be zero in LBP and 3.23 g/m 3 in HBP); (2) emissions during diesel consumption: 0.0214 kg/L CO, 0.0866 kg/L NOx, 2.71E- 5 kg/L VOC, 5.85E-4 kg/L sulfur oxide (SOx) and 0.005 kg/L inhalable particles (PM10) (Kan et al., 2015). The results of pollutant emissions are shown in Table 5 and Fig. 4.
The study considers the energy saving and emission reduction of the biogas production systems, such as avoiding emissions of swine

manure, and avoiding chemical fertilizer, fossil fuel (mainly coal), and thermal power. The saving in coal corresponds to 1.51E+06 FU heat produced by CHP in LBP, 2.23 + 06 MJ/FU biogas (except that for CHP) in LBP, and 2.72 + 06 MJ/FU biogas in HBP, assuming an energy content of 20.908 MJ/kg for coal and a thermal efficiency for coal and biogas stoves of 72% and 57.4%, respectively (Kan et al., 2015; Bruun et al., 2014). The saving in thermal power equals to 1.20E+06 MJ/FU electricity produced in LBP. The saving in chemical fertilizer is based on the nutrient content of the digestate, assuming 46% N, 44% P, and 83% K in chemical fertilizers (Yi et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2010), and assuming 0.15% K in the digestate (Yi et al., 2015) for both systems. The emission factors and calculation results are shown in the Supplementary material (Tables S4 and S5).

framework proposed by the International Organization for Stan- dardization (Wang et al., 2016). The environmental performance of LBP and HBP was assessed following the basic steps, i.e., goal and scope definition, inventory analysis, impact assessment and interpretation.

The goal of LCA is to quantify and compare the energy con- sumption and environmental impacts of LBP and HBP. To compare the biogas production systems, a functional unit (FU) describing the primary function fulfilled by a product system is needed to treat different systems as functionally equivalent (Thomassen et al., 2008). Since the main function of the biogas systems is to pro- duce energy from manure fermentation, 2136 tonne/yr manure flowing into the biogas digester was chosen as the FU of LBP base on its manure processing capacity (equal to ~529 HBPs). In this study, the whole life cycle required for biogas production, from manure leaving the farm to biogas utilization and biogas manure, was considered. The production processes were classified into gath- ering, processing, and use phases (Fig. 3). Transport associated with fertilization was also included. The impacts during the construction and demolition of the systems were not considered here due to the complexity involved with building materials and the lack of basic

Inventory analysis consists of the collection of data on resource use, energy consumption, and pollutant emissions from each phase in the production system (Thomassen et al., 2008). The first step is to determine the feedstock components and manufacturing pro- cesses for each phase. The feedstock for biogas production is swine slurry obtained in raw form. For LBP, manufacturing of swine slurry includes feedstock collection, pretreatment, AD, purification, CHP generation, digestate management and fertilization. For HBP, manufacturing includes feedstock collection, AD, digestate man- agement and fertilization. Further analysis of each phase was car- ried out.

Energy consumption in the LCA of the biogas production sys- items is based on the function of the systems. LBP with a CSTR is complex and functions entirely by mechanical transmission. The energy consumption in the LBP system was estimated using different input and output energy flows, including heating of the feedstock, biogas for CHP, electricity for pumps and mixers, diesel for digestate transport, and heat demand for the reactor, digester and piping. HBP is a traditional biogas production method that depends on manual work and natural fermentation. The energy consumption in the HBP system was estimated mainly using diesel for transport. All energy flows were calculated as uncovered and untransformed natural resources (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006).

Electricity consumption in LBP is based on the CHP generated in the system, assuming that 1 kWh of electricity corresponds to 3.6 MJ of primary energy in the life cycle of LBP. The calorific value of biogas is based on the methane (CH4) content. The calorific value of CH4 is 35.90 MJ/m³; thus, 1 m³ of biogas corresponds to the primary energy of 22.50 I in LBP and 20.57 MJ in HBP. Field investigation showed that the local biogas production was 510-540 tonne-volatile solids (VS) in LBP and 310-380 /tonne-VS in HBP, without consideration of the emissions. These values were similar to the data reported by Wu (2015) in our study area. Thus,
Flow of the system Energy consumption in the system
Fig. 3. System boundaries and main phases in two different biogas production systems (LBP, Large-scale biogas production; HBP, Household biogas production).

the biogas production in LBP (~35 °C) was estimated to be 525 m³/ tonne.VS on average. Biogas produced in HBP was different in each season: 375 m³ 3/tonne-VS in summer (~25 °C 312-375 m³/ tonne-VS in spring and fall (10-20°C), and zero in winter (<10 °C). The average biogas production in HBP was estimated to be 343.75 m³ /tonne VS. The heat demand for AD in LBP was estimated according to Kou et al. (2013) and Doms and Mueller (2005). More details are available in the (Supplementary material S2 and Tables S1-3).

The production and distribution of diesel fuel are estimated to correspond to 10% of the energy content in the fuel (Börjesson, 1996); thus, 1 L of diesel corresponds to 42.65 MJ of primary en- ergy (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006; Jin et al., 2015). Manufacturing and maintenance of trucks and tractors used to transport the digestate and for fertilization are assumed to correspond to 8% and 20% of the energy content in the diesel fuel, respectively (Börjesson, 1996). Thus, using 1 L of diesel for transportation by truck in LBP and tractor in HBP corresponds to 46.06 MJ and 51.18 MJ of primary energy, respectively. The energy inputs and outputs during the life cycle of the biogas production systems are summarized in Table 3.
Choice of allocation involves partitioning the environmental impacts of a multifunctional process. Pollutant emissions during

each process can be divided into direct and indirect emissions. The direct emissions occur during production processes, including manure emissions in the gathering phase, biogas released inad- vertently through leaks in the digester and tubing, and digestate emissions during management and after fertilization in the use phase. (1) Manure emissions of carbon dioxide (CO2) and CH4 ac- count for 85.71% and 14.29% of the carbon loss, respectively (Jiang et al., 2011; Chowdhury et al., 2014), and emissions of nitrogen oxide (NOx) and ammonia (NH3) account for 0.76% and 99.24% of the nitrogen loss, respectively (Chowdhury et al., 2014). In total, 5.49% and 5.99% of the initial total carbon and nitrogen are lost from manure to feedstock in the LBP, and 6.87% and 7.93% are lost in the HBP, respectively; (2) Biogas leakage in the processing phase is calculated to be 0.6% (Xu et al., 2013) and 9.2% (Wang, 2012) of biogas production in LBP and HBP, respectively. (3) Digestate emissions of CO2 and CH4 in management account for 36.6% and 61.4% of the carbon loss in the LBP, and 34.5% and 57.3% in the HBP, respectively (digesters can release biogas from the outlets; Bruun et al., 2014), and emissions of NOx and NH3 account for 0.76% and 99.24% of the nitrogen loss, respectively (ventilation has less in- fluence on nitrogen losses; Chowdhury et al., 2014). In total, 0.61% and 6.45% of the initial total carbon and nitrogen are lost from

feedstock to biogas and digestate in the LBP, and 0.34% and 6.38% are lost in the HBP, respectively. (4) Biogas digestate emissions after fertilization in the use phase are as follows: 0.01% carbon monoxide-carbon (CO-C), 0.25% NOx-N, 0.02% CH4-C, 3.49% CO2-C, 0.07% NH3-N, 2.09% NO-N, and 0.12% PO4-P (of digestate total nutrient content), and 0.018 kg/tonne sulfur dioxide (SO2, of digestate) by referring to the Jin et al. (2015). The direct emission factors are summarized in Table 4.

The indirect emissions occur during energy consumption, including (1) emissions from the burning of biogas: CO2, CO, vola- tile organic compounds (VOC), nitrogen dioxide (NO2), and NOx in LBP (2.96, 0.40, 1930.42, 2.28, and 0.31 g/mi 3 respectively) and HBP (2.76, 0.77, 1808.71, and 0,59 g/m³, 3 respectively) by referring to Jury et al. (2010) and Wang et al. (2010), and SO2 in the two systems (assumed to be zero in LBP and 3.23 g/m 3 in HBP); (2) emissions during diesel consumption: 0.0214 kg/L CO, 0.0866 kg/L NOx, 2.71E- 5 kg/L VOC, 5.85E-4 kg/L sulfur oxide (SOx) and 0.005 kg/L inhalable particles (PM10) (Kan et al., 2015). The results of pollutant emissions are shown in Table 5 and Fig. 4.
The study considers the energy saving and emission reduction of the biogas production systems, such as avoiding emissions of swine

manure, and avoiding chemical fertilizer, fossil fuel (mainly coal), and thermal power. The saving in coal corresponds to 1.51E+06 FU heat produced by CHP in LBP, 2.23 + 06 MJ/FU biogas (except that for CHP) in LBP, and 2.72 + 06 MJ/FU biogas in HBP, assuming an energy content of 20.908 MJ/kg for coal and a thermal efficiency for coal and biogas stoves of 72% and 57.4%, respectively (Kan et al., 2015; Bruun et al., 2014). The saving in thermal power equals to 1.20E+06 MJ/FU electricity produced in LBP. The saving in chemical fertilizer is based on the nutrient content of the digestate, assuming 46% N, 44% P, and 83% K in chemical fertilizers (Yi et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2010), and assuming 0.15% K in the digestate (Yi et al., 2015) for both systems. The emission factors and calculation results are shown in the Supplementary material (Tables S4 and S5).

Energy consumption in the LCA of the biogas production sys- items is based on the function of the systems. LBP with a CSTR is complex and functions entirely by mechanical transmission. The energy consumption in the LBP system was estimated using different input and output energy flows, including heating of the feedstock, biogas for CHP, electricity for pumps and mixers, diesel for digestate transport, and heat demand for the reactor, digester and piping. HBP is a traditional biogas production method that depends on manual work and natural fermentation. The energy consumption in the HBP system was estimated mainly using diesel for transport. All energy flows were calculated as uncovered and untransformed natural resources (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006).

Electricity consumption in LBP is based on the CHP generated in the system, assuming that 1 kWh of electricity corresponds to 3.6 MJ of primary energy in the life cycle of LBP. The calorific value of biogas is based on the methane (CH4) content. The calorific value of CH4 is 35.90 MJ/m³; thus, 1 m³ of biogas corresponds to the primary energy of 22.50 I in LBP and 20.57 MJ in HBP. Field investigation showed that the local biogas production was 510-540 tonne-volatile solids (VS) in LBP and 310-380 /tonne-VS in HBP, without consideration of the emissions. These values were similar to the data reported by Wu (2015) in our study area. Thus,
Flow of the system Energy consumption in the system
Fig. 3. System boundaries and main phases in two different biogas production systems (LBP, Large-scale biogas production; HBP, Household biogas production).

the biogas production in LBP (~35 °C) was estimated to be 525 m³/ tonne.VS on average. Biogas produced in HBP was different in each season: 375 m³ 3/tonne-VS in summer (~25 °C 312-375 m³/ tonne-VS in spring and fall (10-20°C), and zero in winter (<10 °C). The average biogas production in HBP was estimated to be 343.75 m³ /tonne VS. The heat demand for AD in LBP was estimated according to Kou et al. (2013) and Doms and Mueller (2005). More details are available in the (Supplementary material S2 and Tables S1-3).

The production and distribution of diesel fuel are estimated to correspond to 10% of the energy content in the fuel (Börjesson, 1996); thus, 1 L of diesel corresponds to 42.65 MJ of primary en- ergy (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006; Jin et al., 2015). Manufacturing and maintenance of trucks and tractors used to transport the digestate and for fertilization are assumed to correspond to 8% and 20% of the energy content in the diesel fuel, respectively (Börjesson, 1996). Thus, using 1 L of diesel for transportation by truck in LBP and tractor in HBP corresponds to 46.06 MJ and 51.18 MJ of primary energy, respectively. The energy inputs and outputs during the life cycle of the biogas production systems are summarized in Table 3.
Choice of allocation involves partitioning the environmental impacts of a multifunctional process. Pollutant emissions during

each process can be divided into direct and indirect emissions. The direct emissions occur during production processes, including manure emissions in the gathering phase, biogas released inad- vertently through leaks in the digester and tubing, and digestate emissions during management and after fertilization in the use phase. (1) Manure emissions of carbon dioxide (CO2) and CH4 ac- count for 85.71% and 14.29% of the carbon loss, respectively (Jiang et al., 2011; Chowdhury et al., 2014), and emissions of nitrogen oxide (NOx) and ammonia (NH3) account for 0.76% and 99.24% of the nitrogen loss, respectively (Chowdhury et al., 2014). In total, 5.49% and 5.99% of the initial total carbon and nitrogen are lost from manure to feedstock in the LBP, and 6.87% and 7.93% are lost in the HBP, respectively; (2) Biogas leakage in the processing phase is calculated to be 0.6% (Xu et al., 2013) and 9.2% (Wang, 2012) of biogas production in LBP and HBP, respectively. (3) Digestate emissions of CO2 and CH4 in management account for 36.6% and 61.4% of the carbon loss in the LBP, and 34.5% and 57.3% in the HBP, respectively (digesters can release biogas from the outlets; Bruun et al., 2014), and emissions of NOx and NH3 account for 0.76% and 99.24% of the nitrogen loss, respectively (ventilation has less in- fluence on nitrogen losses; Chowdhury et al., 2014). In total, 0.61% and 6.45% of the initial total carbon and nitrogen are lost from

feedstock to biogas and digestate in the LBP, and 0.34% and 6.38% are lost in the HBP, respectively. The indirect emissions occur during energy consumption, including (1) emissions from the burning of biogas: CO2, CO, vola- tile organic compounds (VOC), nitrogen dioxide (NO2), and NOx in LBP (2.96, 0.40, 1930.42, 2.28, and 0.31 g/mi 3 respectively) and HBP (2.76, 0.77, 1808.71, and 0,59 g/m³, 3 respectively) by referring to Jury et al. (2010) and Wang et al. (2010), and SO2 in the two systems (assumed to be zero in LBP and 3.23 g/m 3 in HBP); (2) emissions during diesel consumption: 0.0214 kg/L CO, 0.0866 kg/L NOx, 2.71E- 5 kg/L VOC, 5.85E-4 kg/L sulfur oxide (SOx) and 0.005 kg/L inhalable particles (PM10) (Kan et al., 2015). The results of pollutant emissions are shown in Table 5 and Fig. 4.
The study considers the energy saving and emission reduction of the biogas production systems, such as avoiding emissions of swine

manure, and avoiding chemical fertilizer, fossil fuel (mainly coal), and thermal power. The saving in coal corresponds to 1.51E+06 FU heat produced by CHP in LBP, 2.23 + 06 MJ/FU biogas (except that for CHP) in LBP, and 2.72 + 06 MJ/FU biogas in HBP, assuming an energy content of 20.908 MJ/kg for coal and a thermal efficiency for coal and biogas stoves of 72% and 57.4%, respectively (Kan et al., 2015; Bruun et al., 2014). The saving in thermal power equals to 1.20E+06 MJ/FU electricity produced in LBP. The saving in chemical fertilizer is based on the nutrient content of the digestate, assuming 46% N, 44% P, and 83% K in chemical fertilizers (Yi et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2010), and assuming 0.15% K in the digestate (Yi et al., 2015) for both systems. The emission factors and calculation results are shown in the Supplementary material (Tables S4 and S5).

The goal of LCA is to quantify and compare the energy con- sumption and environmental impacts of LBP and HBP. To compare the biogas production systems, a functional unit (FU) describing the primary function fulfilled by a product system is needed to treat different systems as functionally equivalent (Thomassen et al., 2008). Since the main function of the biogas systems is to pro- duce energy from manure fermentation, 2136 tonne/yr manure flowing into the biogas digester was chosen as the FU of LBP base on its manure processing capacity (equal to ~529 HBPs). In this study, the whole life cycle required for biogas production, from manure leaving the farm to biogas utilization and biogas manure, was considered. The production processes were classified into gath- ering, processing, and use phases (Fig. 3). Transport associated with fertilization was also included. The impacts during the construction and demolition of the systems were not considered here due to the complexity involved with building materials and the lack of basic

feedstock to biogas and digestate in the LBP, and 0.34% and 6.38% are lost in the HBP, respectively. (2010), and SO2 in the two systems (assumed to be zero in LBP and 3.23 g/m 3 in HBP); (2) emissions during diesel consumption: 0.0214 kg/L CO, 0.0866 kg/L NOx, 2.71E- 5 kg/L VOC, 5.85E-4 kg/L sulfur oxide (SOx) and 0.005 kg/L inhalable particles (PM10) (Kan et al., 2015). The study considers the energy saving and emission reduction of the biogas production systems, such as avoiding emissions of swine

manure, and avoiding chemical fertilizer, fossil fuel (mainly coal), and thermal power. The saving in coal corresponds to 1.51E+06 FU heat produced by CHP in LBP, 2.23 + 06 MJ/FU biogas (except that for CHP) in LBP, and 2.72 + 06 MJ/FU biogas in HBP, assuming an energy content of 20.908 MJ/kg for coal and a thermal efficiency for coal and biogas stoves of 72% and 57.4%, respectively (Kan et al., 2015; Bruun et al., 2014). The saving in thermal power equals to 1.20E+06 MJ/FU electricity produced in LBP. The saving in chemical fertilizer is based on the nutrient content of the digestate, assuming 46% N, 44% P, and 83% K in chemical fertilizers (Yi et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2010), and assuming 0.15% K in the digestate (Yi et al., 2015) for both systems. The emission factors and calculation results are shown in the Supplementary material (Tables S4 and S5).

framework proposed by the International Organization for Stan- dardization (Wang et al., 2016). The environmental performance of LBP and HBP was assessed following the basic steps, i.e., goal and scope definition, inventory analysis, impact assessment and interpretation.

The goal of LCA is to quantify and compare the energy con- sumption and environmental impacts of LBP and HBP. To compare the biogas production systems, a functional unit (FU) describing the primary function fulfilled by a product system is needed to treat different systems as functionally equivalent (Thomassen et al., 2008). Since the main function of the biogas systems is to pro- duce energy from manure fermentation, 2136 tonne/yr manure flowing into the biogas digester was chosen as the FU of LBP base on its manure processing capacity (equal to ~529 HBPs). In this study, the whole life cycle required for biogas production, from manure leaving the farm to biogas utilization and biogas manure, was considered. The production processes were classified into gath- ering, processing, and use phases (Fig. 3). Transport associated with fertilization was also included. The impacts during the construction and demolition of the systems were not considered here due to the complexity involved with building materials and the lack of basic

Inventory analysis consists of the collection of data on resource use, energy consumption, and pollutant emissions from each phase in the production system (Thomassen et al., 2008). The first step is to determine the feedstock components and manufacturing pro- cesses for each phase. The feedstock for biogas production is swine slurry obtained in raw form. For LBP, manufacturing of swine slurry includes feedstock collection, pretreatment, AD, purification, CHP generation, digestate management and fertilization. For HBP, manufacturing includes feedstock collection, AD, digestate man- agement and fertilization. Further analysis of each phase was car- ried out.

Energy consumption in the LCA of the biogas production sys- items is based on the function of the systems. LBP with a CSTR is complex and functions entirely by mechanical transmission. The energy consumption in the LBP system was estimated using different input and output energy flows, including heating of the feedstock, biogas for CHP, electricity for pumps and mixers, diesel for digestate transport, and heat demand for the reactor, digester and piping. HBP is a traditional biogas production method that depends on manual work and natural fermentation. The energy consumption in the HBP system was estimated mainly using diesel for transport. All energy flows were calculated as uncovered and untransformed natural resources (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006).

Electricity consumption in LBP is based on the CHP generated in the system, assuming that 1 kWh of electricity corresponds to 3.6 MJ of primary energy in the life cycle of LBP. The calorific value of biogas is based on the methane (CH4) content. The calorific value of CH4 is 35.90 MJ/m³; thus, 1 m³ of biogas corresponds to the primary energy of 22.50 I in LBP and 20.57 MJ in HBP. Field investigation showed that the local biogas production was 510-540 tonne-volatile solids (VS) in LBP and 310-380 /tonne-VS in HBP, without consideration of the emissions. These values were similar to the data reported by Wu (2015) in our study area. Thus,
Flow of the system Energy consumption in the system
Fig. 3. System boundaries and main phases in two different biogas production systems (LBP, Large-scale biogas production; HBP, Household biogas production).

the biogas production in LBP (~35 °C) was estimated to be 525 m³/ tonne.VS on average. Biogas produced in HBP was different in each season: 375 m³ 3/tonne-VS in summer (~25 °C 312-375 m³/ tonne-VS in spring and fall (10-20°C), and zero in winter (<10 °C). The average biogas production in HBP was estimated to be 343.75 m³ /tonne VS. The heat demand for AD in LBP was estimated according to Kou et al. (2013) and Doms and Mueller (2005). More details are available in the (Supplementary material S2 and Tables S1-3).

The production and distribution of diesel fuel are estimated to correspond to 10% of the energy content in the fuel (Börjesson, 1996); thus, 1 L of diesel corresponds to 42.65 MJ of primary en- ergy (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006; Jin et al., 2015). Manufacturing and maintenance of trucks and tractors used to transport the digestate and for fertilization are assumed to correspond to 8% and 20% of the energy content in the diesel fuel, respectively (Börjesson, 1996). Thus, using 1 L of diesel for transportation by truck in LBP and tractor in HBP corresponds to 46.06 MJ and 51.18 MJ of primary energy, respectively. The energy inputs and outputs during the life cycle of the biogas production systems are summarized in Table 3.
Choice of allocation involves partitioning the environmental impacts of a multifunctional process. Pollutant emissions during

each process can be divided into direct and indirect emissions. The direct emissions occur during production processes, including manure emissions in the gathering phase, biogas released inad- vertently through leaks in the digester and tubing, and digestate emissions during management and after fertilization in the use phase. (1) Manure emissions of carbon dioxide (CO2) and CH4 ac- count for 85.71% and 14.29% of the carbon loss, respectively (Jiang et al., 2011; Chowdhury et al., 2014), and emissions of nitrogen oxide (NOx) and ammonia (NH3) account for 0.76% and 99.24% of the nitrogen loss, respectively (Chowdhury et al., 2014). In total, 5.49% and 5.99% of the initial total carbon and nitrogen are lost from manure to feedstock in the LBP, and 6.87% and 7.93% are lost in the HBP, respectively; (2) Biogas leakage in the processing phase is calculated to be 0.6% (Xu et al., 2013) and 9.2% (Wang, 2012) of biogas production in LBP and HBP, respectively. (3) Digestate emissions of CO2 and CH4 in management account for 36.6% and 61.4% of the carbon loss in the LBP, and 34.5% and 57.3% in the HBP, respectively (digesters can release biogas from the outlets; Bruun et al., 2014), and emissions of NOx and NH3 account for 0.76% and 99.24% of the nitrogen loss, respectively (ventilation has less in- fluence on nitrogen losses; Chowdhury et al., 2014). In total, 0.61% and 6.45% of the initial total carbon and nitrogen are lost from

framework proposed by the International Organization for Stan- dardization (Wang et al., 2016). The environmental performance of LBP and HBP was assessed following the basic steps, i.e., goal and scope definition, inventory analysis, impact assessment and interpretation.

The goal of LCA is to quantify and compare the energy con- sumption and environmental impacts of LBP and HBP. To compare the biogas production systems, a functional unit (FU) describing the primary function fulfilled by a product system is needed to treat different systems as functionally equivalent (Thomassen et al., 2008). Since the main function of the biogas systems is to pro- duce energy from manure fermentation, 2136 tonne/yr manure flowing into the biogas digester was chosen as the FU of LBP base on its manure processing capacity (equal to ~529 HBPs). In this study, the whole life cycle required for biogas production, from manure leaving the farm to biogas utilization and biogas manure, was considered. The production processes were classified into gath- ering, processing, and use phases (Fig. 3). Transport associated with fertilization was also included. The impacts during the construction and demolition of the systems were not considered here due to the complexity involved with building materials and the lack of basic

Inventory analysis consists of the collection of data on resource use, energy consumption, and pollutant emissions from each phase in the production system (Thomassen et al., 2008). The first step is to determine the feedstock components and manufacturing pro- cesses for each phase. The feedstock for biogas production is swine slurry obtained in raw form. For LBP, manufacturing of swine slurry includes feedstock collection, pretreatment, AD, purification, CHP generation, digestate management and fertilization. For HBP, manufacturing includes feedstock collection, AD, digestate man- agement and fertilization. Further analysis of each phase was car- ried out.

Energy consumption in the LCA of the biogas production sys- items is based on the function of the systems. LBP with a CSTR is complex and functions entirely by mechanical transmission. The energy consumption in the LBP system was estimated using different input and output energy flows, including heating of the feedstock, biogas for CHP, electricity for pumps and mixers, diesel for digestate transport, and heat demand for the reactor, digester and piping. HBP is a traditional biogas production method that depends on manual work and natural fermentation. The energy consumption in the HBP system was estimated mainly using diesel for transport. All energy flows were calculated as uncovered and untransformed natural resources (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006).

Electricity consumption in LBP is based on the CHP generated in the system, assuming that 1 kWh of electricity corresponds to 3.6 MJ of primary energy in the life cycle of LBP. The calorific value of biogas is based on the methane (CH4) content. The calorific value of CH4 is 35.90 MJ/m³; thus, 1 m³ of biogas corresponds to the primary energy of 22.50 I in LBP and 20.57 MJ in HBP. Field investigation showed that the local biogas production was 510-540 tonne-volatile solids (VS) in LBP and 310-380 /tonne-VS in HBP, without consideration of the emissions. These values were similar to the data reported by Wu (2015) in our study area. Thus,
Flow of the system Energy consumption in the system
Fig. 3. System boundaries and main phases in two different biogas production systems (LBP, Large-scale biogas production; HBP, Household biogas production).

the biogas production in LBP (~35 °C) was estimated to be 525 m³/ tonne.VS on average. Biogas produced in HBP was different in each season: 375 m³ 3/tonne-VS in summer (~25 °C 312-375 m³/ tonne-VS in spring and fall (10-20°C), and zero in winter (<10 °C). The average biogas production in HBP was estimated to be 343.75 m³ /tonne VS. The heat demand for AD in LBP was estimated according to Kou et al. (2013) and Doms and Mueller (2005). More details are available in the (Supplementary material S2 and Tables S1-3).

The production and distribution of diesel fuel are estimated to correspond to 10% of the energy content in the fuel (Börjesson, 1996); thus, 1 L of diesel corresponds to 42.65 MJ of primary en- ergy (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006; Jin et al., 2015). Manufacturing and maintenance of trucks and tractors used to transport the digestate and for fertilization are assumed to correspond to 8% and 20% of the energy content in the diesel fuel, respectively (Börjesson, 1996). Thus, using 1 L of diesel for transportation by truck in LBP and tractor in HBP corresponds to 46.06 MJ and 51.18 MJ of primary energy, respectively. The energy inputs and outputs during the life cycle of the biogas production systems are summarized in Table 3.
Choice of allocation involves partitioning the environmental impacts of a multifunctional process. Pollutant emissions during

each process can be divided into direct and indirect emissions. The direct emissions occur during production processes, including manure emissions in the gathering phase, biogas released inad- vertently through leaks in the digester and tubing, and digestate emissions during management and after fertilization in the use phase. (1) Manure emissions of carbon dioxide (CO2) and CH4 ac- count for 85.71% and 14.29% of the carbon loss, respectively (Jiang et al., 2011; Chowdhury et al., 2014), and emissions of nitrogen oxide (NOx) and ammonia (NH3) account for 0.76% and 99.24% of the nitrogen loss, respectively (Chowdhury et al., 2014). In total, 5.49% and 5.99% of the initial total carbon and nitrogen are lost from manure to feedstock in the LBP, and 6.87% and 7.93% are lost in the HBP, respectively; (2) Biogas leakage in the processing phase is calculated to be 0.6% (Xu et al., 2013) and 9.2% (Wang, 2012) of biogas production in LBP and HBP, respectively. (3) Digestate emissions of CO2 and CH4 in management account for 36.6% and 61.4% of the carbon loss in the LBP, and 34.5% and 57.3% in the HBP, respectively (digesters can release biogas from the outlets; Bruun et al., 2014), and emissions of NOx and NH3 account for 0.76% and 99.24% of the nitrogen loss, respectively (ventilation has less in- fluence on nitrogen losses; Chowdhury et al., 2014). In total, 0.61% and 6.45% of the initial total carbon and nitrogen are lost from

feedstock to biogas and digestate in the LBP, and 0.34% and 6.38% are lost in the HBP, respectively. (4) Biogas digestate emissions after fertilization in the use phase are as follows: 0.01% carbon monoxide-carbon (CO-C), 0.25% NOx-N, 0.02% CH4-C, 3.49% CO2-C, 0.07% NH3-N, 2.09% NO-N, and 0.12% PO4-P (of digestate total nutrient content), and 0.018 kg/tonne sulfur dioxide (SO2, of digestate) by referring to the Jin et al. (2015). The direct emission factors are summarized in Table 4.

The indirect emissions occur during energy consumption, including (1) emissions from the burning of biogas: CO2, CO, vola- tile organic compounds (VOC), nitrogen dioxide (NO2), and NOx in LBP (2.96, 0.40, 1930.42, 2.28, and 0.31 g/mi 3 respectively) and HBP (2.76, 0.77, 1808.71, and 0,59 g/m³, 3 respectively) by referring to Jury et al. (2010) and Wang et al. (2010), and SO2 in the two systems (assumed to be zero in LBP and 3.23 g/m 3 in HBP); (2) emissions during diesel consumption: 0.0214 kg/L CO, 0.0866 kg/L NOx, 2.71E- 5 kg/L VOC, 5.85E-4 kg/L sulfur oxide (SOx) and 0.005 kg/L inhalable particles (PM10) (Kan et al., 2015). The results of pollutant emissions are shown in Table 5 and Fig. 4.
The study considers the energy saving and emission reduction of the biogas production systems, such as avoiding emissions of swine

manure, and avoiding chemical fertilizer, fossil fuel (mainly coal), and thermal power. The saving in coal corresponds to 1.51E+06 FU heat produced by CHP in LBP, 2.23 + 06 MJ/FU biogas (except that for CHP) in LBP, and 2.72 + 06 MJ/FU biogas in HBP, assuming an energy content of 20.908 MJ/kg for coal and a thermal efficiency for coal and biogas stoves of 72% and 57.4%, respectively (Kan et al., 2015; Bruun et al., 2014). The saving in thermal power equals to 1.20E+06 MJ/FU electricity produced in LBP. The saving in chemical fertilizer is based on the nutrient content of the digestate, assuming 46% N, 44% P, and 83% K in chemical fertilizers (Yi et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2010), and assuming 0.15% K in the digestate (Yi et al., 2015) for both systems. The emission factors and calculation results are shown in the Supplementary material (Tables S4 and S5).

The calorific value of CH4 is 35.90 MJ/m³; thus, 1 m³ of biogas corresponds to the primary energy of 22.50 I in LBP and 20.57 MJ in HBP. Field investigation showed that the local biogas production was 510-540 tonne-volatile solids (VS) in LBP and 310-380 /tonne-VS in HBP, without consideration of the emissions. These values were similar to the data reported by Wu (2015) in our study area. Thus,
Flow of the system Energy consumption in the system

the biogas production in LBP (~35 °C) was estimated to be 525 m³/ tonne.VS on average. Biogas produced in HBP was different in each season: 375 m³ 3/tonne-VS in summer (~25 °C 312-375 m³/ tonne-VS in spring and fall (10-20°C), and zero in winter (<10 °C). The average biogas production in HBP was estimated to be 343.75 m³ /tonne VS. The heat demand for AD in LBP was estimated according to Kou et al. (2013) and Doms and Mueller (2005). The production and distribution of diesel fuel are estimated to correspond to 10% of the energy content in the fuel (Börjesson, 1996); thus, 1 L of diesel corresponds to 42.65 MJ of primary en- ergy (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006; Jin et al., 2015). Manufacturing and maintenance of trucks and tractors used to transport the digestate and for fertilization are assumed to correspond to 8% and 20% of the energy content in the diesel fuel, respectively (Börjesson, 1996). Thus, using 1 L of diesel for transportation by truck in LBP and tractor in HBP corresponds to 46.06 MJ and 51.18 MJ of primary energy, respectively. The energy inputs and outputs during the life cycle of the biogas production systems are summarized in Table 3.
Choice of allocation involves partitioning the environmental impacts of a multifunctional process. Pollutant emissions during

each process can be divided into direct and indirect emissions. The direct emissions occur during production processes, including manure emissions in the gathering phase, biogas released inad- vertently through leaks in the digester and tubing, and digestate emissions during management and after fertilization in the use phase. (1) Manure emissions of carbon dioxide (CO2) and CH4 ac- count for 85.71% and 14.29% of the carbon loss, respectively (Jiang et al., 2011; Chowdhury et al., 2014), and emissions of nitrogen oxide (NOx) and ammonia (NH3) account for 0.76% and 99.24% of the nitrogen loss, respectively (Chowdhury et al., 2014). In total, 5.49% and 5.99% of the initial total carbon and nitrogen are lost from manure to feedstock in the LBP, and 6.87% and 7.93% are lost in the HBP, respectively; (2) Biogas leakage in the processing phase is calculated to be 0.6% (Xu et al., 2013) and 9.2% (Wang, 2012) of biogas production in LBP and HBP, respectively. (3) Digestate emissions of CO2 and CH4 in management account for 36.6% and 61.4% of the carbon loss in the LBP, and 34.5% and 57.3% in the HBP, respectively (digesters can release biogas from the outlets; Bruun et al., 2014), and emissions of NOx and NH3 account for 0.76% and 99.24% of the nitrogen loss, respectively (ventilation has less in- fluence on nitrogen losses; Chowdhury et al., 2014). In total, 0.61% and 6.45% of the initial total carbon and nitrogen are lost from

(2010) and Wang et al. (2010), and SO2 in the two systems (assumed to be zero in LBP and 3.23 g/m 3 in HBP); (2) emissions during diesel consumption: 0.0214 kg/L CO, 0.0866 kg/L NOx, 2.71E- 5 kg/L VOC, 5.85E-4 kg/L sulfur oxide (SOx) and 0.005 kg/L inhalable particles (PM10) (Kan et al., 2015). The results of pollutant emissions are shown in Table 5 and Fig. 4.
The study considers the energy saving and emission reduction of the biogas production systems, such as avoiding emissions of swine

manure, and avoiding chemical fertilizer, fossil fuel (mainly coal), and thermal power. The saving in coal corresponds to 1.51E+06 FU heat produced by CHP in LBP, 2.23 + 06 MJ/FU biogas (except that for CHP) in LBP, and 2.72 + 06 MJ/FU biogas in HBP, assuming an energy content of 20.908 MJ/kg for coal and a thermal efficiency for coal and biogas stoves of 72% and 57.4%, respectively (Kan et al., 2015; Bruun et al., 2014). The saving in thermal power equals to 1.20E+06 MJ/FU electricity produced in LBP. The saving in chemical fertilizer is based on the nutrient content of the digestate, assuming 46% N, 44% P, and 83% K in chemical fertilizers (Yi et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2010), and assuming 0.15% K in the digestate (Yi et al., 2015) for both systems. The emission factors and calculation results are shown in the Supplementary material (Tables S4 and S5).

framework proposed by the International Organization for Stan- dardization (Wang et al., 2016). The environmental performance of LBP and HBP was assessed following the basic steps, i.e., goal and scope definition, inventory analysis, impact assessment and interpretation.

The goal of LCA is to quantify and compare the energy con- sumption and environmental impacts of LBP and HBP. To compare the biogas production systems, a functional unit (FU) describing the primary function fulfilled by a product system is needed to treat different systems as functionally equivalent (Thomassen et al., 2008). Since the main function of the biogas systems is to pro- duce energy from manure fermentation, 2136 tonne/yr manure flowing into the biogas digester was chosen as the FU of LBP base on its manure processing capacity (equal to ~529 HBPs). In this study, the whole life cycle required for biogas production, from manure leaving the farm to biogas utilization and biogas manure, was considered. The production processes were classified into gath- ering, processing, and use phases (Fig. 3). Transport associated with fertilization was also included. The impacts during the construction and demolition of the systems were not considered here due to the complexity involved with building materials and the lack of basic

Inventory analysis consists of the collection of data on resource use, energy consumption, and pollutant emissions from each phase in the production system (Thomassen et al., 2008). The first step is to determine the feedstock components and manufacturing pro- cesses for each phase. The feedstock for biogas production is swine slurry obtained in raw form. For LBP, manufacturing of swine slurry includes feedstock collection, pretreatment, AD, purification, CHP generation, digestate management and fertilization. For HBP, manufacturing includes feedstock collection, AD, digestate man- agement and fertilization. Further analysis of each phase was car- ried out.

Energy consumption in the LCA of the biogas production sys- items is based on the function of the systems. LBP with a CSTR is complex and functions entirely by mechanical transmission. The energy consumption in the LBP system was estimated using different input and output energy flows, including heating of the feedstock, biogas for CHP, electricity for pumps and mixers, diesel for digestate transport, and heat demand for the reactor, digester and piping. HBP is a traditional biogas production method that depends on manual work and natural fermentation. The energy consumption in the HBP system was estimated mainly using diesel for transport. All energy flows were calculated as uncovered and untransformed natural resources (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006).

Electricity consumption in LBP is based on the CHP generated in the system, assuming that 1 kWh of electricity corresponds to 3.6 MJ of primary energy in the life cycle of LBP. The calorific value of biogas is based on the methane (CH4) content. The calorific value of CH4 is 35.90 MJ/m³; thus, 1 m³ of biogas corresponds to the primary energy of 22.50 I in LBP and 20.57 MJ in HBP. Field investigation showed that the local biogas production was 510-540 tonne-volatile solids (VS) in LBP and 310-380 /tonne-VS in HBP, without consideration of the emissions. These values were similar to the data reported by Wu (2015) in our study area. Thus,

(4) Biogas digestate emissions after fertilization in the use phase are as follows: 0.01% carbon monoxide-carbon (CO-C), 0.25% NOx-N, 0.02% CH4-C, 3.49% CO2-C, 0.07% NH3-N, 2.09% NO-N, and 0.12% PO4-P (of digestate total nutrient content), and 0.018 kg/tonne sulfur dioxide (SO2, of digestate) by referring to the Jin et al. (2015). The direct emission factors are summarized in Table 4.

The indirect emissions occur during energy consumption, including (1) emissions from the burning of biogas: CO2, CO, vola- tile organic compounds (VOC), nitrogen dioxide (NO2), and NOx in LBP (2.96, 0.40, 1930.42, 2.28, and 0.31 g/mi 3 respectively) and HBP (2.76, 0.77, 1808.71, and 0,59 g/m³, 3 respectively) by referring to Jury et al. (2010) and Wang et al. (2010), and SO2 in the two systems (assumed to be zero in LBP and 3.23 g/m 3 in HBP); (2) emissions during diesel consumption: 0.0214 kg/L CO, 0.0866 kg/L NOx, 2.71E- 5 kg/L VOC, 5.85E-4 kg/L sulfur oxide (SOx) and 0.005 kg/L inhalable particles (PM10) (Kan et al., 2015). The results of pollutant emissions are shown in Table 5 and Fig. 4.
The study considers the energy saving and emission reduction of the biogas production systems, such as avoiding emissions of swine

manure, and avoiding chemical fertilizer, fossil fuel (mainly coal), and thermal power. The saving in coal corresponds to 1.51E+06 FU heat produced by CHP in LBP, 2.23 + 06 MJ/FU biogas (except that for CHP) in LBP, and 2.72 + 06 MJ/FU biogas in HBP, assuming an energy content of 20.908 MJ/kg for coal and a thermal efficiency for coal and biogas stoves of 72% and 57.4%, respectively (Kan et al., 2015; Bruun et al., 2014). The saving in thermal power equals to 1.20E+06 MJ/FU electricity produced in LBP. The saving in chemical fertilizer is based on the nutrient content of the digestate, assuming 46% N, 44% P, and 83% K in chemical fertilizers (Yi et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2010), and assuming 0.15% K in the digestate (Yi et al., 2015) for both systems. The emission factors and calculation results are shown in the Supplementary material (Tables S4 and S5).

framework proposed by the International Organization for Stan- dardization (Wang et al., 2016). The environmental performance of LBP and HBP was assessed following the basic steps, i.e., goal and scope definition, inventory analysis, impact assessment and interpretation.

The goal of LCA is to quantify and compare the energy con- sumption and environmental impacts of LBP and HBP. To compare the biogas production systems, a functional unit (FU) describing the primary function fulfilled by a product system is needed to treat different systems as functionally equivalent (Thomassen et al., 2008). Since the main function of the biogas systems is to pro- duce energy from manure fermentation, 2136 tonne/yr manure flowing into the biogas digester was chosen as the FU of LBP base on its manure processing capacity (equal to ~529 HBPs). In this study, the whole life cycle required for biogas production, from manure leaving the farm to biogas utilization and biogas manure, was considered. The production processes were classified into gath- ering, processing, and use phases (Fig. 3). Transport associated with fertilization was also included. The impacts during the construction and demolition of the systems were not considered here due to the complexity involved with building materials and the lack of basic

Inventory analysis consists of the collection of data on resource use, energy consumption, and pollutant emissions from each phase in the production system (Thomassen et al., 2008). The first step is to determine the feedstock components and manufacturing pro- cesses for each phase. The feedstock for biogas production is swine slurry obtained in raw form. For LBP, manufacturing of swine slurry includes feedstock collection, pretreatment, AD, purification, CHP generation, digestate management and fertilization. For HBP, manufacturing includes feedstock collection, AD, digestate man- agement and fertilization. Further analysis of each phase was car- ried out.

Energy consumption in the LCA of the biogas production sys- items is based on the function of the systems. LBP with a CSTR is complex and functions entirely by mechanical transmission. The energy consumption in the LBP system was estimated using different input and output energy flows, including heating of the feedstock, biogas for CHP, electricity for pumps and mixers, diesel for digestate transport, and heat demand for the reactor, digester and piping. HBP is a traditional biogas production method that depends on manual work and natural fermentation. The energy consumption in the HBP system was estimated mainly using diesel for transport. All energy flows were calculated as uncovered and untransformed natural resources (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006).

Electricity consumption in LBP is based on the CHP generated in the system, assuming that 1 kWh of electricity corresponds to 3.6 MJ of primary energy in the life cycle of LBP. The calorific value of biogas is based on the methane (CH4) content. The calorific value of CH4 is 35.90 MJ/m³; thus, 1 m³ of biogas corresponds to the primary energy of 22.50 I in LBP and 20.57 MJ in HBP. Field investigation showed that the local biogas production was 510-540 tonne-volatile solids (VS) in LBP and 310-380 /tonne-VS in HBP, without consideration of the emissions. These values were similar to the data reported by Wu (2015) in our study area. Thus,
Flow of the system Energy consumption in the system

the biogas production in LBP (~35 °C) was estimated to be 525 m³/ tonne.VS on average. Biogas produced in HBP was different in each season: 375 m³ 3/tonne-VS in summer (~25 °C 312-375 m³/ tonne-VS in spring and fall (10-20°C), and zero in winter (<10 °C). The average biogas production in HBP was estimated to be 343.75 m³ /tonne VS. The heat demand for AD in LBP was estimated according to Kou et al. (2013) and Doms and Mueller (2005). More details are available in the (Supplementary material S2 and Tables S1-3).

The production and distribution of diesel fuel are estimated to correspond to 10% of the energy content in the fuel (Börjesson, 1996); thus, 1 L of diesel corresponds to 42.65 MJ of primary en- ergy (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006; Jin et al., 2015). Manufacturing and maintenance of trucks and tractors used to transport the digestate and for fertilization are assumed to correspond to 8% and 20% of the energy content in the diesel fuel, respectively (Börjesson, 1996). Thus, using 1 L of diesel for transportation by truck in LBP and tractor in HBP corresponds to 46.06 MJ and 51.18 MJ of primary energy, respectively. The energy inputs and outputs during the life cycle of the biogas production systems are summarized in Table 3.
Choice of allocation involves partitioning the environmental impacts of a multifunctional process. Pollutant emissions during

each process can be divided into direct and indirect emissions. The direct emissions occur during production processes, including manure emissions in the gathering phase, biogas released inad- vertently through leaks in the digester and tubing, and digestate emissions during management and after fertilization in the use phase. (1) Manure emissions of carbon dioxide (CO2) and CH4 ac- count for 85.71% and 14.29% of the carbon loss, respectively (Jiang et al., 2011; Chowdhury et al., 2014), and emissions of nitrogen oxide (NOx) and ammonia (NH3) account for 0.76% and 99.24% of the nitrogen loss, respectively (Chowdhury et al., 2014). In total, 5.49% and 5.99% of the initial total carbon and nitrogen are lost from manure to feedstock in the LBP, and 6.87% and 7.93% are lost in the HBP, respectively; (2) Biogas leakage in the processing phase is calculated to be 0.6% (Xu et al., 2013) and 9.2% (Wang, 2012) of biogas production in LBP and HBP, respectively. (3) Digestate emissions of CO2 and CH4 in management account for 36.6% and 61.4% of the carbon loss in the LBP, and 34.5% and 57.3% in the HBP, respectively (digesters can release biogas from the outlets; Bruun et al., 2014), and emissions of NOx and NH3 account for 0.76% and 99.24% of the nitrogen loss, respectively (ventilation has less in- fluence on nitrogen losses; Chowdhury et al., 2014). In total, 0.61% and 6.45% of the initial total carbon and nitrogen are lost from

37: Table 1 shows that LBP had a longer performance period and lifetime with a higher production rate of high-calorie biogas compared with HBP. Proper maintenance in LBP allowed the equipment to work much more efficiently with a longer operating life. The waste heat and some biogas were used to ensure the re- action temperature in production to maintain a high rate of biogas production (525 m³ /tonne-VS). LBP produced biogas with a higher CH4 content (61%, Table 1), making the caloric value of biogas 22.50 MJ/m³.

42: Table 3 shows that total energy input in LBP was 35.89 times higher than in HBP. In LBP, heat for AD was the main energy input, accounting for 64% of the total. Consumption of electricity and biogas respectively accounted for 12% and 18% of the total, while diesel and vehicle accounted for 6% of the total for digestate transport. Higher degree of automation in LBP required relatively high energy input in the processing phase (94% of the total, Fig. 5). Energy input to the processing phase in LBP was dependent on the heat demand for AD and the electricity for plant operation. The electricity was serviced from the generation capacity of the plant

per se (e.g., CHP), while the heat demand was satisfied from CHP (87%) and biogas consumption (13%). The single mode of heating by CHP was not sufficient for the heat demand of fermentation when air temperature was lower than 16.5 °C. Therefore, LBP required an input of 17,144.46 /FU extra biogas for heat per year, equivalent to 17% of the biogas output for cooking. Fossil fuel consumed in the systems included diesel and vehicle used for transport of digestate. As the transport radius of LBP was ten times larger than that for HBP, the energy input of fossil fuel was higher for the former than for the latter.

Table 3 also shows that the total energy output of LBP was 82% higher than that of HBP. The energy output of LBP was more diverse, with biogas, electricity (24% consumed in the plant), and heat. In HBP, there was only biogas. The biogas produced by AD in LBP (5.58E+06 MJ/FU) was twice as much as that in HBP, 60% of which was used for CHP, 7% used for heat in the processing phase, and 33% injected into the biogas pipeline for cooking. Therefore, the net output of biogas in LBP was two thirds of that in HBP.
