45: As Table 9 shows, the total environmental impact index of HBP (329.24) was 13% higher than that of LBP (290.83). Herein, the environmental burdens considered involved all inputs and outputs required in each phase. The gathering phase played a minor role in the environmental impacts concerning GWP, AP, EP, and POP, The processing phase arose as an important source of GWP, POP, and HTP in LBP, and a minor source of GWP and POP in HBP. The use phase was the main source of all potential impacts in LBP (except POP) and HBP (Fig. 5). The major contributor to the environmental impact during production processes was EP, accounting for 78% and 79% of the total impact in the two systems. AP contributed less to the environmental impact (16% and 17% of the total), followed by GWP (5% and 3% of the total). EC, POP, and HTP had little contri- bution to the environmental impact. According to the source of emissions, the three phases were further divided into six stages: feedstock collection, AD, biogas combustion, digestate manage- ment, fertilization, and transport. Different potential impacts showed different features in each stage.
46: For LBP, GWP was 1.33 times that of HBP (Table 8). In both systems, GWP was mainly concentrated in the processing and use phases (Fig. 5). The main contributors to GWP were the emissions of CO2 (70% in LBP and 53% in HBP) and CH4 (27% in LBP and 44% in HBP) (Fig. 6). The total CO2 emission from LBP was 1.74 times higher

than that of HBP (Table 5). Biogas combustion was a significant source of CO2 emissions (87% in LBP and 76% in HBP), which involved CHP, heat supply, and biogas consumption in the LBP. CO2 emissions from feedstock collection, digestate management, and fertilization totally contributed 13% and 21% to the total CO2 loss in LBP and HBP, respectively (Fig. 4).

The total CH4 emission from LBP was 18% lower than that of HBP (Table 5). In LBP, the CH4 emissions arose predominantly from feedstock collection (27%) and digestate management (67%). In HBP, biogas leakage of AD was the most significant source of CH4 emissions (45%), followed by digestate management (32%) and feedstock collection (23%; Fig. 4). The CH4 emission from biogas leakage of AD in HBP was 8.35 times higher than that of LBP, leading to a higher GWP during AD (1.24E+05 kg CO2-equiv./FU in HBP and 1.49E+04 kg CO2-equiv./FU in LBP). In addition, the CH4 loss from digestate management and fertilizer in use phase of LBP was 1.69 times higher than that of HBP.

Taken together, the impact in terms of GWP was mainly attrib- uted to biogas combustion (62% in LBP and 41% in HBP), and natural volatilization of polluting gases during feedstock collection and digestate utilization (35% in LBP and 37% in HBP). Biogas leakage of AD contributed 21% to the total GWP in HBP (Fig. 7).

3.3.2. Acidification and eutrophication LBP showed 18% lower AP and 15% lower EP than did HBP (Table 8). Both AP and EP were concentrated in the use phase (Fig. 5). NH3 emissions were the greatest contributor to AP (96% in
|Biogas combustion |Digestate transport |Digestate fertilization |Digestate management |Anaerobic digestion |Feedstock collection
Fig. 7. Relative contributions of different stages to environmental impacts of two different biogas production systems.

LBP and 94% in HBP) and EP (89% in LBP and 90% in HBP), which were concentrated in digestate management (93% in LBP and 91% in HBP) in the use phase, with less than 10% from feedstock collection in the gathering phase (Fig. 6). The emission of NH3 in LBP was 14% lower than that in HBP. AP and EP were mainly due to natural volatilization of manure and digestate in the two systems (96-99%, Fig. 7).

The CH4 emission from biogas leakage of AD in HBP was 8.35 times higher than that of LBP, leading to a higher GWP during AD (1.24E+05 kg CO2-equiv./FU in HBP and 1.49E+04 kg CO2-equiv./FU in LBP). In addition, the CH4 loss from digestate management and fertilizer in use phase of LBP was 1.69 times higher than that of HBP.

Taken together, the impact in terms of GWP was mainly attrib- uted to biogas combustion (62% in LBP and 41% in HBP), and natural volatilization of polluting gases during feedstock collection and digestate utilization (35% in LBP and 37% in HBP). Biogas leakage of AD contributed 21% to the total GWP in HBP (Fig. 7).

The emission of NH3 in LBP was 14% lower than that in HBP. AP and EP were mainly due to natural volatilization of manure and digestate in the two systems (96-99%, Fig. 7).

than that of HBP (Table 5). Biogas combustion was a significant source of CO2 emissions (87% in LBP and 76% in HBP), which involved CHP, heat supply, and biogas consumption in the LBP. LBP and 94% in HBP) and EP (89% in LBP and 90% in HBP), which were concentrated in digestate management (93% in LBP and 91% in HBP) in the use phase, with less than 10% from feedstock collection in the gathering phase (Fig. 6). 49: Table 8 shows that the POP of LBP was 49% higher than that of HBP. POP was mainly concentrated in the processing and use phases in LBP, and the use phase in HBP (Fig. 5). VOC emissions were the greatest contributor to POP: 79% in LBP and 59% in HBP. This was followed by CH4, which contributed 14% and 26% to POP in LBP and HBP, respectively (Fig. 6). The VOC emissions in the systems were mainly produced during the combustion of biogas. The VOC emissions in LBP occurred intensely in the processing phase, ac- counting for 67% of the total, with 33% in the use phase, whereas the VOC emissions in HBP only occurred in the use phase (Fig. 4). Thus, the POP of the systems was mainly due to the combustion of biogas: 84% in LBP and 72% in HBP (Fig. 7).
50: Table 8 shows that the HTP of HBP was 2.26 times as much as that of LBP. HTP was mainly concentrated in the processing and use phases in LBP, and the use phase in HBP (Fig. 5). In LBP, NO2 made the greatest contribution to HTP, accounting for 67% of the total, with 32% from SO2. In HBP, SO2 made the greatest contribution to HTP, accounting for 70% of the total, with 30% from NO2 (Fig. 6). NO2 emissions in the systems mainly occurred during the combustion of biogas, similar to VOC emissions. The SO2 emission of HBP was 4.9 times higher than that of LBP. Owing to the use of technical desulfurization equipment (>95% efficiency), low SO2 emissions in LBP mainly occurred during digestate fertilization (99%) and were

ignored during biogas combustion in this study. In HBP, desulfur- ization was conducted through desulfurizers with short cycle time and low efficiency; hence, high SO2 emissions in HBP mainly occurred during biogas combustion (79%), with 21% from digestate fertilization (Fig. 4). In total, biogas combustion contributed 67% to the HTP in LBP and 85% in HBP (Fig. 6).
51: <header>3.4. Efficiency of energy saving and emission reduction </header>

AD of 2136 tonnes of pig manure helped saving 47.02 tonnes of fertilizer, 3.34E+5 kWh of thermal power, and 185.17 tonnes of coal in LBP. It also saved 51.01 tonnes of fertilizer and 103.69 tonnes of coal in HBP. Meanwhile, AD reduced CH4 and NH3 emissions during manure management. These contributed to a large reduction in the environmental impact index of -376.60 in LBP and -408.01 in HBP, 78% for EP and 18% for AP in the two systems on average (Table 9).

From Table 8, the avoiding of fuel by biogas and chemical fer- tilizer by digestate reduced the direct energy consumption for coal and electricity and the indirect energy consumption for chemical fertilizer, saving 9.75E+06 MJ of energy in LBP and 4.88E+06 MJ in HBP. The main environmental impacts of biogas production in terms of EP, AP, and GWP were reduced mainly by the avoiding of manure emissions (95%, 91%, and 46% in LBP; 97%, 94%, and 66% in HBP, respectively). The reduction in POP mainly came from manure emissions avoided (52% in LBP and 65% in HBP). The avoiding of chemical fertilizer mainly reduced the emissions of CO2, CH4, SO2, NOx, NH3, and VOC, and thus considerably reduced GWP, POP, and HTP in the two systems (21%, 10%, and 81% in LBP; 32%, 16%, and 41% in HBP, respectively). The avoiding of fuel reduced large emissions of CO2, CH4, SO2, and NOx, and thus considerably reduced GWP (44%), POP (14%), and HTP (189%) in LBP and HTP (25%) in HBP.

53: LCA is a quantitative procedure to evaluate the environmental burdens associated with processes and identify the opportunities for obtaining environmental advantages, allowing decision makers to be correctly informed (Cremiato et al., 2017). According to the LCA of the biogas production systems, pollutant emissions can occur during both the upstream and downstream processes of AD and biogas production as well as from biogas per se, causing envi- ronmental impacts (Lijó et al., 2014). Herein, LCA was used to gain insight into the environmental performance of modern LBP versus traditional HBP and identify their potential for optimizing biogas production method.
54: <header>4.1. Effect of biogas production systems on energy balance </header>

In this study, the LBP output more energy under mesophilic (30-40°C) conditions than HBP at ambient temperatures restricted by the environment. Digestion at higher temperatures has many advantages, such as higher specific growth rate for anaerobic mi- croorganisms and a higher kinetic advantage in fermentation (Liu et al., 2017). These advantages can improve solid-liquid separa- tion and the stability of organic wastes, which are positively related to the stability of biogas production and the content of CH4 in the biogas (Liu et al., 2017; Ahring et al., 2001).

The highest production of biogas in HBP reached tonne.V in summer (ambient temperatures of 18-29 reaction temperatures of 20-25 while no biogas was produced in winter due to low temperatures. Cold winter in north China results in the low efficiency of biogas production compared with the warmer summer months; thus, biogas can only partly substitute for the total coal for cooking of a household over the course of a year and mainly in summer (Ding et al., 2012). The rate of biogas production in LBP under mesophilic (35 °C conditions was almost twice as much as that in HBP at ambient temperatures. Kavitha et al. (2017) have found that anaerobic biodegradation at the optimal temper- ature (40 °C) can promote an increase of 1.6 times in the degree of cell lysis and CH4 production potential. In addition, the solid con- centration in LBP (7.2%) is higher than that in HBP (6.3%), and there is a positive correlation between solid concentration (5-8%) and CH4production from pig waste (Aboudi et al., 2017). Therefore, LBP systems always use mesophilic conditions with a suitable solid concentration (~8% according to Aboudi et al., 2017 and Pei et al., 2011) to guarantee the rate of biogas production and increase the content of CH4 in biogas, producing a higher output of energy as a result.

The efficiency of biogas utilization and the performance period per year are factors impacting the performance of biogas produc- tion systems (Pöschl et al., 2012; Pei et al., 2011). In the present study, LBP showed higher biogas utilization efficiency than HBP. The improved efficiency of biogas utilization in LBP can be attrib- uted to CHP. According to Grando et al. (2017), the most frequent activity is power generation around the subjects related to the use of biogas. Additionally, the utilization of waste heat for biogas po- wer generation is a fast-developing and widely used heating technology (Kou et al., 2013) that can meet 89% of the heat demand in LBP during digestion, helping to equilibrate the imbalance of biogas production in summer and winter, which extends the per- formance period per year of LBP. Biogas-to-CHP is the most com- mon utilization in Germany with the efficiency of 83-88% for biogas consumption, where energy input for feedstock sterilization and heating digesters can be fulfilled by the generation capacity of the plant per se (Pöschl et al., 2012). In another study, a CHP unit was operated during the day working hours to satisfy the high

electricity load, and a boiler to furnish the thermal power during the night hours (Ragazzi et al., 2017). In LBP, however, heat from CHP was higher than the demand only in summer months, leading to extra biogas consumption for heat in most of the year.

58: <header>4.2. Effect of biogas production systems on environmental emissions </header>

In the present study, the ranges of GWP, AP, EP, POP, and HTP during the LCA of the systems were estimated to be 275.86-366.87 CO2-equiv., 8.34-9.84kg SO2-equiv., 1.67-1.921 PO4-equiv., 0.13-0.2 C2H4-equiv., and 0.06-0.15 kg 1,4-DCB per tonne manure for biogas production, respectively, without consideration of environmental emissions avoided. When considering the emissions avoided, the avoiding of manure man- agement, chemical fertilizer, and fuel consumption would sharply limit the environmental impacts of GWP, AP, EP, POP, and HTP in biogas production systems. AD shows great environmental per- formance when considering the absent emissions of bad odors and the reduced fossil fuel and chemical fertilizer (Ardolino et al., 2018; Edwards et al., 2017). Comparing with other studies using these impact categories revealed remarkable differences. Pei (2012) ignored pollutant emissions during the gathering phase and digestate management, and reported the GWP, AP, EP, and POP of 143.14kg CO2-equiv., 0.023 kg SO2-equiv., 0.001 kg PO4-equiv., and 0.000861 C2H4-equiv. per tonne pig manure for AD, respectively. De Vries et al. (2012) considered diffuse emissions derived from digestate treatment and mitigation emissions from avoided manure management, chemical fertilizer and energy, and obtained the GWP of CO2-equiv. and AP of 0.09 kg SO2-equiv. of the mono-digestion per tonne pig manure. The assessment results are influenced by the consideration or not within the system bound- aries of the diffuse emissions derived from each process and the mitigation emissions from avoided activities, which contributed to the differences across different studies.

The environmental impacts of biogas production selected in this study were mainly affected by CO2 emissions derived from biogas consumption and NH3 emissions from digestate management. Similarly, Lijó et al. (2014) found that emissions derived from biogas consumption (CO2) and digestate application (e.g., NH3 and CH4) were important sources of environmental impacts in terms of GWP, AP, EP, and POP during electricity production from anaerobic co- digestion of pig slurry and energy crops. However, Börjesson and Berglund (2007) showed that uncontrolled CH4 emission during AD and digestate management, NOx emission during manure storage and mineral fertilizer, and nitrate and NH3 emissions from cropping and wastes handling were significant factors for the environmental impacts of GWP, AP, and EP in biogas production. Moreover, Edwards et al. (2017) indicated that the collection pro- cess played a minor role in GWP and had a significant effect on HTP based on an evaluation of the environmental impacts of anaerobic co-digestion of food waste and sewage sludge. In conclusion, dif- ferences in the feedstock used, feedstock processed, and system boundaries result in differences in the final assessment results of the main emissions during biogas production (Lijó et al., 2014). Among all the environmental impacts during the LCA of the systems in this study, EP was found to be the most significant contributor to the total environmental impact assessment results, followed by AP. NH3 emissions were the main cause of EP and AP, and were concentrated in the use phase of both systems. Manure has a low C/N ratio that will lead to high ammonia concentration in the digester (Grando et al., 2017). During digestion, NH3 can be synthesized via organic acids in the digestate to ammonium salts, making it easy for NH3 and NOx emissions to be produced after

digestion (Marcato et al., 2009; Martines et al., 2010). The NH3 emissions in HBP were much greater than in LBP, resulting in a higher EP and AP, due to the lack of storage and sedimentation. Storage and sedimentation before fertilization can reduce the ni- trogen content in solid grains of digestate, which can reduce the amount of nitrogen flowing into farmlands and hence the emission of NH3 after fertilization (Jin et al., 2015).

GWP is a general assessment factor for the environmental impact of biogas, and is mainly caused by CO2 emissions from biogas combustion and uncontrolled CH4 emissions from pumping leakage and digestate storage (Lijó et al., 2014; De Vries et al., 2012). The higher GWP in LBP was mainly due to higher CO2 emissions from biogas combustion. Bruun et al. (2014) indicated that the break-even point at which the released CH4 has as great an impact on GWP as the fuel that has been replaced is between 3% and 51% of the released biogas, depending on the type of fuel that has been replaced. In the present study, CH4 emission was the second most important contributor to GWP after CO2. The CH4 emission mainly occurred during AD and digestate storage in HBP and digestate storage in LBP. A higher leaking rate during biogas production resulted in more CH4 emissions in HBP compared with LBP.

Furthermore, the VOC emissions from biogas combustion were the main cause of POP in the biogas production systems, similar to the study of Wang et al. (2010). The main reason for the higher values of POP in LBP was the higher VOC emissions during the combustion of more biogas. As reported by Cremiato et al. (2017), the increasing of the photo-oxidation impact during AD of the municipal solid waste is due to the biogas combustion. SO2 and NO2 are inorganic substances with HTP (Huijbregts et al., 2000). In the present study, the SO2 and NO2 emissions in HBP were greater than those in LBP through the combustion of per cubic meter biogas. The equipment used for biogas purification in LBP can remove sulfide and other pollutants in biogas, with a desulfurization efficiency of up to 90-100% (Miltner et al., 2017). In HBP, this is mainly done by sulfur elimination (iron oxide) with an unstable purification rate depending on the effective sulfur capacity, as elemental sulfur can block the active surface (Miltner et al., 2017). Consequently, more impurities (e.g., sulfide and nitride) are formed in the biogas product of HBP, which can be burned and turned into SO2 and NO2 and discharged into the air.

In summary, the LBP outputs more energy and performs better in biogas purification; however, the high biogas production results in high GWP and POP, with considerable energy consumption in production processes. The HBP requires small investment and is suitable for households, but its disadvantages include high leakage rate of biogas tank, poor performance of biogas purification, and high AP, EP, and HTP. With the intensive development of animal husbandry, there is a trend for developing efficient plants at large scales, which needs financial resources and environment-friendly technologies (Ragazzi et al., 2017).

(2014) found that emissions derived from biogas consumption (CO2) and digestate application (e.g., NH3 and CH4) were important sources of environmental impacts in terms of GWP, AP, EP, and POP during electricity production from anaerobic co- digestion of pig slurry and energy crops. However, Börjesson and Berglund (2007) showed that uncontrolled CH4 emission during AD and digestate management, NOx emission during manure storage and mineral fertilizer, and nitrate and NH3 emissions from cropping and wastes handling were significant factors for the environmental impacts of GWP, AP, and EP in biogas production. Moreover, Edwards et al. (2017) indicated that the collection pro- cess played a minor role in GWP and had a significant effect on HTP based on an evaluation of the environmental impacts of anaerobic co-digestion of food waste and sewage sludge. In conclusion, dif- ferences in the feedstock used, feedstock processed, and system boundaries result in differences in the final assessment results of the main emissions during biogas production (Lijó et al., 2014). Among all the environmental impacts during the LCA of the systems in this study, EP was found to be the most significant contributor to the total environmental impact assessment results, followed by AP. NH3 emissions were the main cause of EP and AP, and were concentrated in the use phase of both systems. Manure has a low C/N ratio that will lead to high ammonia concentration in the digester (Grando et al., 2017). During digestion, NH3 can be synthesized via organic acids in the digestate to ammonium salts, making it easy for NH3 and NOx emissions to be produced after

digestion (Marcato et al., 2009; Martines et al., 2010). The NH3 emissions in HBP were much greater than in LBP, resulting in a higher EP and AP, due to the lack of storage and sedimentation. Storage and sedimentation before fertilization can reduce the ni- trogen content in solid grains of digestate, which can reduce the amount of nitrogen flowing into farmlands and hence the emission of NH3 after fertilization (Jin et al., 2015).

GWP is a general assessment factor for the environmental impact of biogas, and is mainly caused by CO2 emissions from biogas combustion and uncontrolled CH4 emissions from pumping leakage and digestate storage (Lijó et al., 2014; De Vries et al., 2012). The higher GWP in LBP was mainly due to higher CO2 emissions from biogas combustion. Bruun et al. (2014) indicated that the break-even point at which the released CH4 has as great an impact on GWP as the fuel that has been replaced is between 3% and 51% of the released biogas, depending on the type of fuel that has been replaced. In the present study, CH4 emission was the second most important contributor to GWP after CO2. The CH4 emission mainly occurred during AD and digestate storage in HBP and digestate storage in LBP. A higher leaking rate during biogas production resulted in more CH4 emissions in HBP compared with LBP.

Furthermore, the VOC emissions from biogas combustion were the main cause of POP in the biogas production systems, similar to the study of Wang et al. (2010). The main reason for the higher values of POP in LBP was the higher VOC emissions during the combustion of more biogas. As reported by Cremiato et al. (2017), the increasing of the photo-oxidation impact during AD of the municipal solid waste is due to the biogas combustion. SO2 and NO2 are inorganic substances with HTP (Huijbregts et al., 2000). In the present study, the SO2 and NO2 emissions in HBP were greater than those in LBP through the combustion of per cubic meter biogas. The equipment used for biogas purification in LBP can remove sulfide and other pollutants in biogas, with a desulfurization efficiency of up to 90-100% (Miltner et al., 2017). In HBP, this is mainly done by sulfur elimination (iron oxide) with an unstable purification rate depending on the effective sulfur capacity, as elemental sulfur can block the active surface (Miltner et al., 2017). Consequently, more impurities (e.g., sulfide and nitride) are formed in the biogas product of HBP, which can be burned and turned into SO2 and NO2 and discharged into the air.

In summary, the LBP outputs more energy and performs better in biogas purification; however, the high biogas production results in high GWP and POP, with considerable energy consumption in production processes. The HBP requires small investment and is suitable for households, but its disadvantages include high leakage rate of biogas tank, poor performance of biogas purification, and high AP, EP, and HTP. With the intensive development of animal husbandry, there is a trend for developing efficient plants at large scales, which needs financial resources and environment-friendly technologies (Ragazzi et al., 2017).
60: <header>4.2. Effect of biogas production systems on environmental emissions </header>

In the present study, the ranges of GWP, AP, EP, POP, and HTP during the LCA of the systems were estimated to be 275.86-366.87 CO2-equiv., 8.34-9.84kg SO2-equiv., 1.67-1.921 PO4-equiv., 0.13-0.2 C2H4-equiv., and 0.06-0.15 kg 1,4-DCB per tonne manure for biogas production, respectively, without consideration of environmental emissions avoided. When considering the emissions avoided, the avoiding of manure man- agement, chemical fertilizer, and fuel consumption would sharply limit the environmental impacts of GWP, AP, EP, POP, and HTP in biogas production systems. AD shows great environmental per- formance when considering the absent emissions of bad odors and the reduced fossil fuel and chemical fertilizer (Ardolino et al., 2018; Edwards et al., 2017). Comparing with other studies using these impact categories revealed remarkable differences. Pei (2012) ignored pollutant emissions during the gathering phase and digestate management, and reported the GWP, AP, EP, and POP of 143.14kg CO2-equiv., 0.023 kg SO2-equiv., 0.001 kg PO4-equiv., and 0.000861 C2H4-equiv. per tonne pig manure for AD, respectively. (2012) considered diffuse emissions derived from digestate treatment and mitigation emissions from avoided manure management, chemical fertilizer and energy, and obtained the GWP of CO2-equiv. and AP of 0.09 kg SO2-equiv. of the mono-digestion per tonne pig manure. 64: According to Chen et al. (2017), the total annual production of biogas in China reached 15.5 billion m³ in 2014, with 13.24 billion m³ in rural areas and an annual household production of 373 m³; there were 103,036 agricultural waste disposal projects producing 2 billion m³ of biogas and 320 industrial waste disposal projects producing 0.25 billion m³ of biogas. The large amount of biogas produced would have good energy-saving and emission-reducing effect. However, HBPs are constrained by limited funding and technological support and have shortcomings such as low comprehensive use rate and high scrap rate of biogas tanks (Chen et al., 2017). LBPs, alternatively, can increase the production rate
and the plants' benefits, showing great development and applica- tion prospects.

Considering the energy balance and environmental impacts, LBP is the more optimal approach to livestock decontamination and resource recovery. LBP requires extra biogas, which accounts for only 14% of the total calorific requirements to maintain the reaction temperature, which decreases the biogas final production by 22%. The heating system in LBP can be improved by using solar heating rather than extra biogas (16,401.54 m of biogas per year) to meet the increasing heat demand at lower air temperatures. Wang et al. (2017) have shown that a solar greenhouse for a 1200 --digester can save ~6 tonne of coal per year while maintaining the temper- ature of AD. In addition, biogas-to-CHP is not the best use of biogas when considering environmental load; the production of bio- methane for road transport is always cleaner than the production of energy (Ardolino et al., 2018) and the former requires high costs of biogas upgrading (Grando et al., 2017). With the development of biogas upgrading technologies, biogas-to-biomethane would be a good choice for the LBPs.

Poor stability of biogas production (more in summer and less in autumn and spring) and undeveloped management limit the per- formance of HBP. Biogas production by a single HBP system failed to satisfy continuous power generation (Liu, 2007). Combined with the space structure of villages in Tongchuan, it is a practical option to build HBPs in tandem to achieve generous biogas production. The waste heat from power generation could be used for biogas production. Furthermore, AD can be more efficient by simulta- neously digesting with different substrates than digesting with one substrate (Grando et al., 2017; Edwards et al., 2017), and some pre- treatment methods are reported to increase the anaerobic biode- gradability of substrates (Martínez-Valdez et al., 2017; Grando et al., 2017). The environmental impacts of these methods need to be assessed in the future research.

Uncertainties in this study arise mainly from the estimation of environmental emissions from feedstock collection, digestate management, and digestate fertilization. In general, these emis- sions are difficult to measure (Gutiérrez et al., 2016).
65: According to Chen et al. (2017), the total annual production of biogas in China reached 15.5 billion m³ in 2014, with 13.24 billion m³ in rural areas and an annual household production of 373 m³; there were 103,036 agricultural waste disposal projects producing 2 billion m³ of biogas and 320 industrial waste disposal projects producing 0.25 billion m³ of biogas. The large amount of biogas produced would have good energy-saving and emission-reducing effect. However, HBPs are constrained by limited funding and technological support and have shortcomings such as low comprehensive use rate and high scrap rate of biogas tanks (Chen et al., 2017). LBPs, alternatively, can increase the production rate
and the plants' benefits, showing great development and applica- tion prospects.

Considering the energy balance and environmental impacts, LBP is the more optimal approach to livestock decontamination and resource recovery. LBP requires extra biogas, which accounts for only 14% of the total calorific requirements to maintain the reaction temperature, which decreases the biogas final production by 22%. The heating system in LBP can be improved by using solar heating rather than extra biogas (16,401.54 m of biogas per year) to meet the increasing heat demand at lower air temperatures. Wang et al. (2017) have shown that a solar greenhouse for a 1200 --digester can save ~6 tonne of coal per year while maintaining the temper- ature of AD. In addition, biogas-to-CHP is not the best use of biogas when considering environmental load; the production of bio- methane for road transport is always cleaner than the production of energy (Ardolino et al., 2018) and the former requires high costs of biogas upgrading (Grando et al., 2017). With the development of biogas upgrading technologies, biogas-to-biomethane would be a good choice for the LBPs.

Poor stability of biogas production (more in summer and less in autumn and spring) and undeveloped management limit the per- formance of HBP. Biogas production by a single HBP system failed to satisfy continuous power generation (Liu, 2007). Combined with the space structure of villages in Tongchuan, it is a practical option to build HBPs in tandem to achieve generous biogas production. The waste heat from power generation could be used for biogas production. Furthermore, AD can be more efficient by simulta- neously digesting with different substrates than digesting with one substrate (Grando et al., 2017; Edwards et al., 2017), and some pre- treatment methods are reported to increase the anaerobic biode- gradability of substrates (Martínez-Valdez et al., 2017; Grando et al., 2017). The environmental impacts of these methods need to be assessed in the future research.

Uncertainties in this study arise mainly from the estimation of environmental emissions from feedstock collection, digestate management, and digestate fertilization. In general, these emis- sions are difficult to measure (Gutiérrez et al., 2016).

(2017), the total annual production of biogas in China reached 15.5 billion m³ in 2014, with 13.24 billion m³ in rural areas and an annual household production of 373 m³; there were 103,036 agricultural waste disposal projects producing 2 billion m³ of biogas and 320 industrial waste disposal projects producing 0.25 billion m³ of biogas. (2017) have shown that a solar greenhouse for a 1200 --digester can save ~6 tonne of coal per year while maintaining the temper- ature of AD. Furthermore, AD can be more efficient by simulta- neously digesting with different substrates than digesting with one substrate (Grando et al., 2017; Edwards et al., 2017), and some pre- treatment methods are reported to increase the anaerobic biode- gradability of substrates (Martínez-Valdez et al., 2017; Grando et al., 2017). 67: Based on the LCA, both large-scale (LBP) and household biogas production (HBP) plants showed good environmental sustainability in pollutant emission reduction and clean energy manufacture. The LBP had a better environmental performance than HBP in terms of energy use and environmental impacts. The complexity of the biogas production system and the use of co-products contributed to the better performance of LBP in terms of the performance period, CH4 production rate, and biogas utilization efficiency. However, higher CO2 and VOC emissions in LBP resulted in greater potentials for global warming and photochemical oxidation, whereas HBP showed higher potentials for acidification, eutrophication, and human toxicity mainly due to higher NH3 and SO2 emissions. These potential environmental impacts mainly arose from biogas com- bustion and digestate management in the use phase. Consequently, research is needed to gain better understanding about cleaner new uses of biogas and environment-friendly pre-treatment processes for maximizing biogas production. Environment-friendly technol- logies are required for promoting biogas production and utilization processes, in order to reduce the environmental impacts and improve the utilization efficiency of biogas. To sum up, plant at a large scale shows improved environmental efficiency compared with that at a household scale and has a great potential to be the main mode of biogas production in the future.
